steps.title: Identify the sentence structure
steps.content: The word “assembles” is the main verb of the sentence. The blank fills in what follows “Richard,” and must introduce a modifier — not a second independent clause — because the sentence already has a main verb. A participial phrase set off by commas on both sides is the grammatically correct structure here.

steps.title: Eliminate three choices, confirm one
steps.content:
• Choice A — Serra is intending: Introduces a finite verb (“is intending”) that creates a second independent clause. Two finite verbs without a conjunction or semicolon produce a comma splice. Violated rule: comma splice. ✗
  • Choice B — Serra, intends: Places a comma between the subject (“Serra”) and the finite verb “intends,” separating subject from verb with a comma — ungrammatical. Violated rule: comma between subject and predicate. ✗
  • Choice C — Serra, intending: Uses the present participle “intending,” forming a supplementary participial phrase set off by commas. This allows “assembles” to function as the sole main verb without ambiguity. ✓
  • Choice D — Serra intends: Introduces a second finite verb (“intends”) alongside “assembles” without any conjunction, creating a run-on sentence. Violated rule: fused independent clauses. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
